content.full_text:
alifornia
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 27,
1993
Janice Rogers Brown
Legal Affairs Secretary
Office of the Governor
State Capitol
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1-93-137
Dear Ms.
Brown:
You have requested advice concerning the reporting of various
public events held during the week of March 22,
1993, to honor the
memory and accomplishments of the late Assemblyman B.
T. Collins
under the provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act).
1
-/
Your letter asks only a general question..
Therefore,
we consider
it to be a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c)
2/
Your letter states that the events following Assemblyman
Collins’
death were funded by conations from individuals and
businesses,
and asks if the Act’s reporting provisions would be
applicable to such donations.
As you know, the Act requires disclosure by public officials
of gifts and other benefits received if an official receives gifts
totaling $50 or more from a single source.
In addition,
elected
officials and certain administrative officials may not receive
gifts totaling more than $270
iii a calendar year from a single
source,
and may not receive qifts totaling more than $10 in a
calendar month made or arranged by a lobbyist.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
42S j Street, Suite S0O
•
P.O. Box S07
•
Sacramento, CA
95S04-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


• ers Brown
No.
1—93—137
According to information provided by lobbyist Paula Treat,
the California Correctional Peace Officers Association paid $5,000
and later reimbursed her an additional $2,564.05 for costs
associated with a “wake” held during the week following
Assemblyman Collins’ death.
The event was open to the public,
and
Ms.
Treat estimates that 2,500 to 3,000 people attended.
It
appears that there wera no oth2r events at which officials
received reportable benefits.
Although we agree.that these payments were made to honor
Assemblyman Collins and were not made for the purpose of
entertaining or making gifts to officials,
the Act does not
provide a method for exempting such payments from disclosure or
limitations.
However,
because the amount of benefit attributable
to each attendee is approximately $3.00
($7,564.05 divided by
2,500), the benefit will not be reportable by any official who
attended the event unless the official receives other gifts from
the California Correctional Peace Officers Association during 1993
totaling $47 or more.
If you have additional questions concerning this matter,
please do not hesitate to contact me at
(916)
322-5662.
Sincerely,
uJ
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

sections.question: Your letter states that the events following Assemblyman Collins' death were funded by donations from individuals and businesses, and asks if the Act's reporting provisions would be applicable to such donations.
sections.conclusion: Although we agree that these payments were made to honor Assemblyman Collins and were not made for the purpose of entertaining or making gifts to officials, the Act does not provide a method for exempting such payments from disclosure or limitations. However, because the amount of benefit attributable to each attendee is approximately $3.00 ($7,564.05 divided by 2,500), the benefit will not be reportable by any official who attended the event unless the official receives other gifts from the California Correctional Peace Officers Association during 1993 totaling $47 or more.
sections.question_synthetic: Are donations funding public memorial events for a deceased assemblyman subject to the Political Reform Act's gift reporting and limitation requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Memorial event donations are technically subject to gift reporting requirements under the Act, but the per-attendee value (approximately $3.00) falls below the $50 reporting threshold, so no reporting is required unless the donor makes additional gifts to officials totaling $47 or more during the year.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter; no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers, but clear Q&A structure present. Letter dated May 27, 1993 regarding memorial events held in March 1993. Document provides specific dollar amounts and threshold calculations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your letter states that the events following Assemblyman Collins' death were funded by donations from individuals and businesses, and asks if the Act's reporting provisions would be applicable to such donations.

CONCLUSION: Although we agree that these payments were made to honor Assemblyman Collins and were not made for the purpose of entertaining or making gifts to officials, the Act does not provide a method for exempting such payments from disclosure or limitations. However, because the amount of benefit attributable to each attendee is approximately $3.00 ($7,564.05 divided by 2,500), the benefit will not be reportable by any official who attended the event unless the official receives other gifts from the California Correctional Peace Officers Association during 1993 totaling $47 or more.

The event was open to the public, and Ms. Treat estimates that 2,500 to 3,000 people attended. It appears that there wera no oth2r events at which officials received reportable benefits. Although we agree.that these payments were made to honor Assemblyman Collins and were not made for the purpose of entertaining or making gifts to officials, the Act does not provide a method for exempting such payments from disclosure or limitations. However, because the amount of benefit attributable to each attendee is approximately $3.00 ($7,564.05 divided by 2,500), the benefit will not be reportable by any official who attended the event unless the official receives other gifts from the California Correctional Peace Officers Association during 1993 totaling $47 or more. embedding.summary: The FPPC provides informal advice on whether donations funding memorial events for deceased Assemblyman B.T. Collins must be reported under the Political Reform Act's gift disclosure rules. The FPPC concludes that while such payments are technically subject to the Act, the low per-attendee benefit amount means no reporting is required unless additional gifts from the same source exceed certain thresholds.
